item.title: US Considers Tapping Oil Under Military Bases to Refill Reserve
item.description: The Trump administration is studying using oil under land at US military bases and other Department of War sites to help refill the nation’s depleted emergency reserves, according to a person familiar with the matter.

item.title: Microsoft Reconsiders Clean-Energy Pledge Made Harder by AI
item.description: The tech giant may drop one of the industry’s most ambitious climate targets

item.title: Microsoft May Abandon Its Target for Powering Data Centers With Clean Energy
item.description: Microsoft Corp. may shelve one of the industry’s most ambitious clean-energy targets as it tries to remove hurdles that could hold it back in the race to power data centers, according to people with knowledge of the matter.

item.title: India’s ReNew Energy Trims Solar Output Due to Grid Constraints
item.description: ReNew Energy Global Plc is being forced to curb its solar power output, putting profits at risk, as India’s inadequate grid infrastructure struggles to absorb renewables during peak generation hours.

item.title: Chinese Truck Drivers Are Going Electric as Gas Prices Soar
item.description: As electric car sales soften in China after years of breakneck growth, electric trucks are taking off, with the emerging industry expected to get a boost from the Iran war.

item.title: Japan Offers Energy Loan, Ammonia Technology to South Africa
item.description: Japan plans to fast-track talks with South Africa on a yen-denominated energy loan, while promoting its technology to blend ammonia with coal to reduce carbon emissions in a country that still generates most of its electricity from one of the dirtiest fossil fuels.

item.title: AEP Weighs Break From Two of America’s Biggest Power Grids
item.description: American Electric Power Co. is reviewing its memberships in two of the biggest US power networks after the utility’s top boss accused the grid operators of being too slow to connect data-center customers.

item.title: Microplastics in the Air Are Making Earth Hotter, Study Finds
item.description: The tiny particles trap heat in the atmosphere, exacerbating climate change.

item.title: SEC Rule to End Biden-Era Climate Policy Sent to White House
item.description: The White House is reviewing a proposal from the Securities and Exchange Commission to formally end Biden-era climate disclosure rules for publicly-traded companies.

item.title: Germany Offers €5 Billion to Help Heavy Industries Cut Emissions
item.description: Germany kicked off an auction to offer up to €5 billion ($5.8 billion) to help heavy industries cut their carbon emissions, easing uncertainty over whether the government would press ahead with the planned subsidies.

item.title: Insurer QBE Faces Investor Pressure Over Extreme Weather Risks
item.description: Australian Ethical Investment Ltd., a pension fund with about A$14 billion ($10 billion) under management, is pressuring QBE Insurance Group Ltd. over the potential threat extreme weather poses to future profitability.

item.title: Iran Shock Boosts South Korea’s Push to Cut Fossil Fuel Imports
item.description: The Iran-driven energy shock is adding urgency to South Korea’s push to cut its reliance on imported fossil fuels, giving President Lee Jae Myung momentum to push his clean-energy agenda.

item.title: AI Boom to Add Hidden Extra Cost to Northeast US Power Bills
item.description: Data centers are driving a surge in carbon prices in the US Northeast, helping push CO2 costs in the region past California and raising the prospect of higher energy costs for consumers.

item.title: Data Center Startup Fermi Ousts CEO After Stock Slides
item.description: The firm plans to power data centers on a grand scale but has struggled to secure clients.

item.title: Heat-Trapping Microplastics Found to Play Role in Climate Change
item.description: The ubiquitous tiny particles absorb sunlight when airborne, contributing to the warming of the planet, according to new research.

item.title: Wildfire Season Starts Early as Drought Fuels Global Fire Risk
item.description: Wildfires are erupting unusually early worldwide, fueled by drought and heat, straining firefighting resources and signaling a longer, more dangerous season ahead.

item.title: South African Farms Face El Niño Drought Risk on Top of Iran War
item.description: South African farmers pinched by mounting costs from the Iran war now face the added risk of an El Niño-induced drought, with implications for lower agricultural output and higher food prices.

item.title: EU Plans to Allocate More Free Carbon Permits to Industry
item.description: The European Commission is set to propose granting 4 billion more free emission permits to the industries covered by the bloc’s carbon market, addressing concerns by some governments and manufacturers over the region’s declining competitiveness.

item.title: Australia to Limit Tax Benefits for EV Buyers to Help Fix Budget
item.description: Australia will limit a tax discount aimed at encouraging purchases of electric vehicles as the center-left government looks for savings in next week’s budget.

item.title: Galvanize Expands Its Strategy of Greening Commercial Real Estate
item.description: Galvanize, the alternative investment manager co-founded by billionaire Tom Steyer, is expanding its commercial real estate portfolio as a war-fueled spike in oil and gas prices supports its strategy of flipping properties after making them more energy efficient.

item.title: EU Proposes to Exclude Leather From Landmark Deforestation Law
item.description: The European Union moved to exclude the leather industry from its landmark law tackling global deforestation as the bloc seeks support from key sectors and countries before the legislation kicks in at year’s end.

item.title: Hedge Fund Fermat Invests in First-of-a-Kind Catastrophe Bonds
item.description: Fermat Capital Management has invested in the Asian Development Bank’s first-ever catastrophe bonds, designed to shield Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan against specific types of natural disasters.

item.title: Spring’s Hot-to-Cold Swings Are Being Juiced by Climate Change
item.description: Abrupt changes in temperature are getting spikier as the planet warms.

item.title: Spring Temperature Whiplash Is Getting Worse With Climate Change
item.description: Abrupt jumps from chilly to warm and back again are typical of the season but becoming more extreme in a warming world.

item.title: BMO Turns to AI and Quantum Computing to Predict Earthquakes
item.description: Bank of Montreal is betting on artificial intelligence and quantum computing to help predict earthquakes and respond to wildfires as part of the Canadian bank’s expansion in the US.

item.title: Nuclear AI Startup Fermi Promised Land and Ample Power. But It Couldn't Sign a Single Client
item.description: Now the ex-CEO is waging a battle for Fermi’s future and its vision of atomic-powered data centers in the Texas panhandle.

item.title: India Braces for Another Summer of Dangerous Heat
item.description: The country is already dealing with energy shortages caused by the Middle East war.

item.title: Warm, Wild Weather Puts Europe on Storm and Drought Watch in May
item.description: Europe is set for weeks of erratic and increasingly warm weather over the coming month, raising the risk of storms, early season drought stress and increasing cooling demand.

item.title: India’s Heat Exposes a Fragile Grid as Energy Crunch Deepens
item.description: India is likely to endure a blistering summer until monsoon rains arrive in June, with higher-than-average heat already straining power grids at a time when the country is grappling with energy shortages.

item.title: Texas Hill Country on Alert for Flooding as Heavy Rains Approach
item.description: A band of severe storms bearing down on central Texas has raised concerns about flooding, with heavy rains expected near the site of last year’s deadly Fourth of July floods.
